mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 3 Series M340d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned B57D30T2 engine typically costs £5,000–£7,500 supply only, with LCI-era MHEV units slightly higher. Fitting adds £1,200–£1,700. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your M340d.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW M340d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the B57 is a modern, well‑engineered straight‑six diesel. It's significantly more reliable than earlier BMW diesels like the N47. The main watchpoints are DPF clogging, EGR carbon build-up, wastegate actuator wear, and AdBlue system faults — all manageable with proper maintenance.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW M340d engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: DPF clogging (short journey use), EGR valve and cooler carbon build-up, turbocharger wastegate actuator wear, AdBlue system faults, and coolant loss. The engine itself is fundamentally robust.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an M340d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. A G20 M340d is a £25,000–£45,000 vehicle — a £6,000 recon is 13–24% of the car's value, making replacement a clear decision. This is well within our 70% BMW scrap threshold.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW M340d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The M340d engine code is B57D30T2 — a 3.0-litre straight-six twin-turbo diesel. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I prevent the common failures on an M340d?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — regular long runs for DPF health, periodic EGR cleaning, and BMW-spec oil changes with shortened intervals (5,000–7,500 miles) are the most effective prevention. If you notice rattling or a drop in power, have the turbo system inspected immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: BMW 3 Series M340d Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current B57D30T2 generation; LCI-era pricing runs slightly higher — see the Era Map above.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a 3 Series M340d engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer).

cta.label: → Compare M340d engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: DPF Clogging (Short Journey Use)
problems.symptoms: DPF warning light, reduced engine performance, increased fuel consumption, engine entering limp mode.
problems.repairCost: £300–£800 (forced regeneration or DPF clean) / £1,500–£3,000 (DPF replacement) [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless DPF is physically damaged)
problems.recommendation: Like all modern diesels, the B57 needs regular long runs for passive DPF regeneration. If your M340d is used primarily for short journeys, plan a 30–45 minute motorway run every two weeks. If the DPF warning light comes on, book a forced regeneration promptly — ignoring it can lead to costly DPF replacement.

problems.title: EGR Valve and Cooler Carbon Build-up
problems.symptoms: Rough idling, reduced engine performance, increased fuel consumption, coolant loss (if cooler fails), engine warning light.
problems.repairCost: £400–£1,000 (EGR cleaning or replacement) [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]
problems.recommendation: The EGR system on the B57 can become clogged with carbon deposits over time. Regular servicing and occasional Italian tune-ups can help. If you notice rough running or a drop in performance, have the EGR system inspected — cleaning it is far cheaper than replacing a failed EGR cooler.

problems.title: Turbocharger Wastegate Actuator Wear
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise from the engine at low speeds or idle, noticeable drop in power, boost pressure loss.
problems.repairCost: £800–£1,500 (wastegate actuator replacement) [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught before turbo damage)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if rattling or power loss occurs

problems.recommendation: The B57's twin-turbo setup delivers exceptional performance, but the wastegate actuator can wear over time. If you hear a rattling noise from the engine bay or notice a drop in power, have the turbo system inspected. Replacing a worn actuator is far cheaper than a full turbo replacement.

buyingChecks.title: Buying This M340d? Check These First
buyIf: * Documented DPF regeneration history or regular long-distance driving
* EGR system cleaned or replaced within the last 40,000 miles
* No rattling from the turbo area — wastegate actuator in good condition
* Full BMW service history with correct oil spec
avoidIf: * DPF warning light with no regeneration history — may indicate heavy clogging
* Rough idle with no EGR history — carbon build-up may be significant
* Rattling from the engine bay — wastegate actuator may be failing
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis — potentially expensive emissions system issues

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The B57 is one of BMW's finest modern diesel engines — a 3.0-litre straight-six with 340 bhp, 700 Nm and genuine M‑lite performance. It's not without its watchpoints — DPF, EGR, wastegate actuator and AdBlue are the main areas to monitor — but it's fundamentally robust. Buy on full service history and pay attention to DPF health. A well‑maintained M340d is a long‑lived performance diesel.

cta.label: → Compare M340d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 3 Series M340d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * DPF Status — Check for warning lights; short journeys can cause clogging
* Cold Start — Listen for rattling; wastegate actuator wear indicator
* Rough Idle — Check for uneven running; EGR carbon build-up possible
* Service History — including which generation (pre‑LCI or LCI) and confirmed maintenance
* Coolant Level — Check for unexplained loss; EGR cooler or water pump may be failing
* AdBlue System — Check for warning lights; AdBlue system faults are common
* Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles?
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures
